title: Lockheed Lands $35B THAAD Mega-Contract as FCAS Collapse Deepens European Fighter Gap
subhead: The Pentagon locks in a seven-year THAAD interceptor deal worth up to $35 billion to quadruple production, while Europe's FCAS sixth-generation fighter program disintegrates — leaving the US with a decade-long head start and Lockheed Martin dominating both missile defense and the export fighter market.

sections.heading: Lockheed Wins $35B THAAD Contract to Quadruple Interceptor Output
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin has secured a seven-year contract worth up to $35 billion to dramatically expand production of Terminal High Altitude Area Defense (THAAD) interceptors, converting a January framework agreement into a long-term procurement plan. The deal aims to quadruple THAAD interceptor production and accelerate deliveries to both the US military and allied nations, according to Lockheed Martin and Interesting Engineering.

The contract is one of the first major multiyear agreements under the Department of War's new buying strategy, which prioritizes demand signaling to give manufacturers confidence to invest in facilities, equipment, and workforce. Lockheed said the deal provides 'the long-term demand needed to boost production and deliver missile defense systems faster.' Tim Cahill, president of Lockheed Martin Missiles and Fire Control, called it 'a transformational shift to multiyear procurement' that will deliver capabilities 'at unprecedented speed and scale.'

Lockheed has already begun building a new Munitions Production Center in Troy, Alabama, part of a broader plan to invest over $9 billion by 2030 in manufacturing expansion. More than 20 new or upgraded facilities have opened across the country, including the Next Generation Interceptor facility in Courtland, Alabama, and the Munitions Acceleration Center in Camden, Arkansas. The THAAD deal follows an April $4.7 billion contract to accelerate PAC-3 MSE interceptor production, part of a broader push that also includes framework agreements for the Precision Strike Missile.
sections.whyItMatters: The $35B THAAD deal signals a structural shift in US missile defense procurement — from incremental purchases to multiyear block buys that guarantee industrial base capacity. It also reflects growing global demand for layered missile defense, particularly from Gulf allies and European NATO members, at a time when THAAD is the only US system capable of intercepting threats both inside and outside Earth's atmosphere.

sections.heading: FCAS Collapse Leaves Europe a Decade Behind US on Sixth-Generation Fighters
sections.body:
Europe's flagship Future Combat Air System (FCAS) program has effectively collapsed after Airbus definitively broke with Dassault, killing the EU's most ambitious military-industrial program this century — estimated at over $100 billion in planned investment. According to El País, the breakup leaves Germany and Spain awaiting further developments while the US extends its already commanding lead in next-generation fighter technology.

Antonio Fonfría, a professor of Applied Economics at the Complutense University of Madrid, estimates that 'the US has an eight- to 10-year head start on us [in Europe] when it comes to the development of fighter jets' — and calls that 'being optimistic.' The gap traces back decades: the F-18 first flew in 1978, while Europe's equivalent Eurofighter didn't fly until 1994. Now Europe has no fifth-generation fighter of its own, making the leap to sixth generation even harder.

Boeing's F-47, the US sixth-generation fighter, is expected in the early 2030s — but is not intended for export, according to Kearney consulting partner Nicolás Sanz, mirroring the F-22's domestic-only policy. What will be exported are 'adjacent systems: companion drones, mission autonomy software, and integration systems.' Meanwhile, 13 European countries have already ordered over 600 F-35s, a number that could reach 700 according to Infodefensa — including Germany, Italy, and the UK. Poland has expressed interest in doubling its order to 64 units. Spain, however, has ruled out purchasing the F-35, despite its carrier being designed for the F-35B variant.

The F-35's success comes despite persistent reliability issues: the full mission capable rate fell from 38% in 2021 to 25% last year, according to the US Government Accountability Office, even as program costs continue to rise.
sections.whyItMatters: The FCAS collapse is not just a program failure — it removes Europe's only credible path to sixth-generation air superiority. With the F-47 restricted to US forces and F-35s filling the gap, European air forces will depend on American platforms for decades, entrenching a dependency that the rearmament debate was supposed to reduce.

sections.heading: South Korea to Train 500,000 'Drone Warriors' as Peninsula Drone Arms Race Intensifies
sections.body:
South Korea's Defense Ministry announced sweeping plans on June 26 to train the country's entire 500,000-strong military as 'drone warriors', making drone operation a 'universal combat tool' and a 'second personal weapon' for every service member, Defense Minister Ahn Gyu-back said in a briefing. The program starts with 11,000 training drones this year, scaling to 60,000 drones deployed across all branches by 2029, with a production target of 110,000 drones by that date, according to Reuters and The Korea Times.

The announcement came the same day North Korea's Kim Jong Un supervised weapons tests and called for a 'deadly and destructive' military posture, evaluating what state media described as a 'special mission' warhead for a tactical ballistic missile. The simultaneous moves underscore a Korean Peninsula drone arms race shaped by lessons from Ukraine and the Middle East.

South Korea's plan faces significant constraints. The country's declining birthrate is shrinking the conscript pool, making it difficult to maintain even the current 450,000 active-duty force — let alone 500,000. The military also mandates 100% domestically produced drone components with no Chinese parts for security reasons, a major challenge given China's dominance of the global commercial drone market through manufacturers like DJI. Min-Cheol Jung of the Team Retriever counter-drone red team warned in War on the Rocks that the 500,000 drone warriors risk becoming 'a hollow force' without sufficient NCO expertise and realistic training.

South Korea's drone operations command is being reorganized away from direct combat authority to focus on collaborating with domestic industry on commercial drone technology development and procurement.
sections.whyItMatters: South Korea's plan is the most ambitious national-scale drone training program announced to date — but its success hinges on industrial capacity for domestic drone production and sustaining a shrinking conscript force. If it works, it creates a template for how numerically inferior militaries can offset manpower gaps through drone ubiquity.

sections.heading: Lockheed Unveils NXGB Hypersonic Glide Body Designed for Mass Production
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin announced on June 24 that it has begun work on its Next Generation Glide Body (NXGB), a hypersonic vehicle designed from the ground up for low-cost, large-scale manufacturing. The program, based at Lockheed's facility in Huntsville, Alabama, targets a first flight in 2027 and represents a shift in US hypersonic weapon strategy: from exquisite, hand-built prototypes to modular, production-ready systems.

The NXGB uses an unpowered glide body architecture — mounted on a rocket booster and launched from ground, ship, or aircraft platforms — rather than more complex powered hypersonic designs. This trades continuous propulsion for manufacturing simplicity, as glide bodies are mechanically simpler to build and deploy while still maintaining speeds above Mach 5. The vehicle features a modular architecture and 'design for manufacturing at scale' approach, drawing on 'decades of lessons learned,' said Johnathon Caldwell, Lockheed's VP for Strategic and Missile Defense Systems.

The announcement follows the US Army's April decision to pivot away from the earlier Common-Hypersonic Glide Body (C-HGB) program toward a more affordable and producible design. Aviation Week reports that the NXGB is intended to be launchable from both air and surface platforms, giving commanders flexibility in deployment.
sections.whyItMatters: The NXGB reflects a maturing US approach to hypersonics: abandoning bespoke prototypes in favor of weapons that can be built in quantity. If Lockheed hits its 2027 flight target and cost goals, it could narrow the production gap with China and Russia, which have fielded operational hypersonic systems ahead of the US.

sections.heading: France Shuts Down Israeli Booths at Eurosatory as Defense-Commerce Tensions Escalate
sections.body:
A dozen Israeli company stands were shut down or boarded up at the Eurosatory defense exhibition outside Paris this week, after the French government barred Israel's defense ministry from participating and restricted Israeli firms to displaying only air defense products — excluding all offensive weapons systems. The restrictions were enforced with temporary walls erected around Israeli exhibits displaying offensive systems, according to Breaking Defense and Anadolu Agency.

Israel's defense ministry called the decision 'disgraceful' and accused France of 'political and commercial calculation,' arguing it 'reeks' of bias and violates 'established norms governing international defense exhibitions.' The move follows a pattern: at the 2025 Paris Air Show, French authorities built wooden walls around Israeli company exhibits, prompting Israeli firms to withdraw on opening day. In 2024, France initially banned Israeli companies from Eurosatory entirely before partially reversing the decision.

The restrictions come amid strained Franco-Israeli relations over Israel's military operations in Lebanon, and after Israel declared earlier in 2026 that it would no longer purchase defense equipment from France. Aviation Week reports that only companies exhibiting missile defense or anti-aircraft systems were allowed to remain open.
sections.whyItMatters: France's escalating restrictions on Israeli defense companies at major arms shows represent a weaponization of exhibition access that goes beyond diplomatic protest — it directly disrupts Israeli defense exports and sets a precedent for politicizing defense trade venues. Israel's retaliatory boycott of French defense purchases signals that this dispute is now affecting real procurement contracts, not just symbolism.

sections.heading: China Researchers Develop Cheap Stealth Coating That Could Enable Mass Production
sections.body:
A research team from Foshan University in China has developed a low-cost microwave-absorbing composite paint using expanded graphite and titanium dioxide — replacing expensive engineered materials like graphene and carbon nanotubes used in current radar-absorbing systems. The research was published in the Journal of Ceramics and reported by NewsBytes.

The composite combines strong electromagnetic wave attenuation with a simple manufacturing process, potentially enabling mass production at dramatically lower costs. The Foshan University team argues this could extend practical applications beyond military stealth technology to civilian uses — including, remarkably, house paint. Multiple outlets including MSN characterized the development as 'military stealth coating sold as cheap house paint,' highlighting the dual-use implications of making radar-absorbing materials widely accessible.

The development comes as China dominates the global commercial drone market and faces growing scrutiny over technology transfer in defense-adjacent sectors. If mass production proves viable, it could reduce the cost barrier for radar-absorbing coatings across not just China's military but potentially export customers as well.
sections.whyItMatters: Stealth coatings have been one of the most expensive and closely guarded elements of fifth-generation fighter programs. If China can mass-produce effective radar-absorbing material at commodity prices, it erodes a key Western technological advantage — and raises proliferation concerns about stealth technology becoming accessible to a wider range of state and non-state actors.

sections.heading: NATO's Arctic Gap: Allies Promised to Secure the Northern Flank, but Readiness Lags
sections.body:
A Reuters investigation published June 26 found that NATO allies who promised Trump they would secure the Arctic 'have got work to do' — reporting from Evenes, Norway, where British and Norwegian soldiers trained in Arctic conditions as part of the Arctic Sentry exercise. The exercise underscores rising tensions with Russia along NATO's northern flank, where Russia has been building major troop deployment infrastructure despite its forces being heavily committed in Ukraine.

Poland's deputy prime minister warned that he 'wouldn't exclude the Russians doing some kind of false flag operation' to justify an attack on NATO, according to CBS News. The warnings come ahead of the NATO summit in Turkey, where the alliance's deputy commander is pushing for the summit to spur increased defense spending commitments.

Meanwhile, the UK has intensified its pressure on Russia's shadow fleet: Royal Marines boarded and seized a Russian tanker in the English Channel carrying 98,000 tonnes of oil, with the ship's captain subsequently charged. The vessel has been anchored off Weymouth since June 14. UK Defense Secretary Dan Jarvis said the interdiction 'deals another blow to Putin' and signals continued enforcement against Russia's sanctions-evading maritime network.
sections.whyItMatters: The Arctic is becoming NATO's most exposed frontier — and the alliance's promised buildup there is proceeding far slower than the rhetoric. Russia's infrastructure investments in the High North continue even while its army is bogged down in Ukraine, suggesting Moscow is positioning for a long-term northern presence that NATO is not yet equipped to contest.

bottomLine: June 27, 2026 marks a day when the defense industry's production capacity became the story: Lockheed Martin locked in a $35B multiyear deal to quadruple THAAD output and unveiled a mass-production hypersonic glide body, South Korea committed to training half a million drone operators, and Chinese researchers published a path to cheap stealth coatings. These are not incremental procurement announcements — they reflect a structural shift toward scale and affordability as the defining metrics of military power. Meanwhile, the FCAS collapse confirmed that Europe's defense industrial base cannot yet produce a fifth-generation fighter, let alone a sixth, deepening dependence on American platforms at exactly the moment the US is restricting their most advanced exports. The day's developments all point in the same direction: whoever can produce fast, cheap, and at scale will set the terms of the next era of military competition.
sources: Interesting Engineering, El País, Reuters, Ars Technica, New Atlas, Aviation Week, Breaking Defense, Anadolu Agency, NewsBytes, MSN, The Korea Times, War on the Rocks, Lockheed Martin press release, US Government Accountability Office, Infodefensa, CBS News, France 24, Le Monde, Globes Israel, Defense One
